Client 2: Yeah, and just… keeping it simple. We’ve got accounts all over the place.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with cash flow—what’s coming in each year. Patricia, what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The total gross is 169950 annually.
Advisor: Thanks. What are the main sources behind that—pension, rental income, anything else?
Client 1: It’s mainly my pension and then rental income we get weekly.

Client 2: We usually say about seventy-five hundred a month, give or take.
Advisor: Okay. When you’re tracking it, what number do you use?
Client 1: Yes—7600 per month is what we use.
Advisor: Perfect. Any debt payments each month—mortgage, car loan, or credit cards you’re carrying?
Client 2: No, we don’t have any of that anymore.

Client 2: Yeah, and just… keeping it simple. We’ve got accounts all over the place.

Client 1: The total gross is 169950 annually.
Advisor: Thanks. What are the main sources behind that—pension, rental income, anything else?
Client 1: It’s mainly my pension and then rental income we get weekly.

Client 1: Monthly debt payments are 0.
Advisor: Good to hear. Let’s talk about what you’ve built—your investable accounts. Roughly what do you consider your investable assets total, excluding the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total 1314300.
Advisor: Thanks. I’ll go account by account and you stop me if I miss something. Do you have a joint taxable account at a bank?
Client 1: Yes, we have a joint taxable account at a bank—758500.

Client 1: Yes, it’s 5400, held at a retirement platform.
Advisor: Understood. Now, for the home—what do you have your primary residence valued at?
Client 1: Correct—1.21M for the primary residence.
Advisor: Thanks. And just to be clear, there aren’t any loans outstanding—no mortgage or other liabilities?
Client 2: None. No loans.

Advisor: Great. That helps a lot—thank you both.
Advisor: Alright—thanks. Before we actually wrap, I realized we didn’t capture a couple of the basic household details. Patricia, William—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids you still support, grandkids, anyone like that?

Client 2: Yeah, and just… keeping it simple. We’ve got accounts all over the place.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with cash flow—what’s coming in each year. Patricia, what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The total gross is 169950 annually.
Advisor: Thanks. What are the main sources behind that—pension, rental income, anything else?
Client 1: It’s mainly my pension and then rental income we get weekly.

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi William—nice to meet you both. Before we dive in, what made you decide to come in now?
Client 1: Thanks. We’re fine day-to-day, but we want a clear plan—especially around taxes and not making a mistake with withdrawals.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… keeping it simple. We’ve got accounts all over the place.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with cash flow—what’s coming in each year. Patricia, what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The total gross is 169950 annually.

Advisor: Okay. Patricia, do you have a retirement account held with an insurance company—like a 401(k) or IRA style account?
Client 1: Yes, in my name—341800, held at an insurance company.
Advisor: Got it. Any other bank or brokerage-style account in your name—something you view through an advisor platform?
Client 1: Yes, I have one held on an advisor platform—208650.
Advisor: Great. And you also mentioned a small private investment—private markets—held on a retirement platform?

Advisor: Okay—thanks. Two last housekeeping items, Patricia and William, and then we’ll let you go. First, just confirming the household: it’s the two of you living together—how many adults in the household?
Client 1: Two adults—2.
Advisor: Great, thank you. Next—risk. When markets swing around, how would you describe your overall comfort level: conservative, moderate, aggressive…?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. I don’t love big drops, but we’re not hiding everything in cash either.
Advisor: Got it. Patricia, does “moderate” fit for you too, or would you put it differently?

Client 2: I always mix that up—are you asking the percent?
Advisor: Yeah, exactly—the percent bracket you tend to fall into, even if it’s just the band.
Client 1: Use 35%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Last one is about how we work together. Our firm is an RIA, a registered investment advisor—does that match what you’re looking for as the advisory channel, versus a brokerage-only relationship?
Client 2: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).

Advisor: Hi Patricia, hi William—nice to meet you both. Before we dive in, what made you decide to come in now?
Client 1: Thanks. We’re fine day-to-day, but we want a clear plan—especially around taxes and not making a mistake with withdrawals.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… keeping it simple. We’ve got accounts all over the place.
Advisor: Totally fair. Let’s start with cash flow—what’s coming in each year. Patricia, what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The total gross is 169950 annually.

Advisor: Yeah, exactly—the percent bracket you tend to fall into, even if it’s just the band.
Client 1: Use 35%.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Last one is about how we work together. Our firm is an RIA, a registered investment advisor—does that match what you’re looking for as the advisory channel, versus a brokerage-only relationship?
Client 2: Yes, RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. And just so I’m tying a bow on today—income we have the pension and the weekly rental income; assets include the joint taxable account at the bank, your retirement-style accounts and the smaller private markets piece, plus the primary residence; liabilities are zero across the board; and we noted Patricia’s disability policy. That still sounds like the full set, right?

Client 1: Retired. Fully retired.
Advisor: Got it. Now, income—earlier we talked through the pension and the rental income, but I want to make sure I have your personal gross annual income number correct in one place. What do you show as your gross annual income, Patricia?
Client 1: It’s 125800 gross annually.
Advisor: Thank you—hang on, I’m just writing that down. And that 125800 is before taxes, right—gross?
Client 1: Yes, before taxes—gross.

Advisor: Perfect. And in terms of the household roles for planning—would you say you’re the primary person I should coordinate with on paperwork and decisions?
Client 1: Yes, I’m the primary.
Advisor: Great. And just to keep the basics clean—employment status for you these days, Patricia: are you fully retired, part-time, anything like that?
